content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Steven M. Danowitz 
Partner, Indirect - State and Local Tax 
Ernst & Young, LLP 
725 S. Figueroa Street 
Los Angeles, CA 90017 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-244 
Dear Mr. Danowitz: 
December 2, 2016 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Mr. Carl Joseph regarding post­
governmental employment provisions of the Political Refonn Act (the "Act").1 Please note that the 
Fair Political Practices Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it provides advice (111 re 
Oglesby ( 1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71 ). 
QUESTION 
ls Mr. Joseph, a former employee of the Franchise Tax Board ("FTB") and currently a 
Principal at Ernst & Young, LLP (hereafter "E&Y"), subject to the permanent ban provisions of 
Sections 87401-87402 in relation to a specific audit that now is subject to protest? 
CONCLUSION 
Based on the limited facts presented, it appears that Sections 87400 et seq. would apply and 
prohibit Mr. Joseph from participating in the audit proceeding. However, Section 87404 does 
provide for a process to allow an agency and former employee to determine factually if a former 
employee should be excluded from a proceeding pursuant to Section 87400 et seq. Section 87404 
does not direct or authorize the Commission to participate in the determination. 
FACTS 
Mr. Joseph worked for the FTB as Counsel in the Multistate Tax Affairs Unit. In this 
position, Mr. Joseph was responsible for coordinating multistate tax policy issues within the Legal 
Division. This included overseeing regulatory and adjudicatory efforts in the multistate arena, as 
well as protests and appeals raising complex multistate tax issues. His duties did not include 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Go,.·emment Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-16-244 
Page No. 2 
supervising any staff. Instead, staff members of the Legal Division were supervised by the Chief 
Counsel and Assistant Chief Counsel Managers, none of which reported to Mr. Joseph, nor did he 
report to the Chief Counsel. His role was to act in a consulting capacity and he reported directly to 
the Executive Officer of the FTB. Mr. Joseph served in this capacity from February of 2012 until he 
left state service in March of 2015. 
E&Y has been engaged by a taxpayer to represent them in a protest of the detenninations 
made by the FTB in an audit. Procedurally, at the conclusion of an audit, the FTB's audit staff 
determines if an assessment is appropriate. If there is an assessment proposed by the audit staff, the 
taxpayer is given the option of protesting the assessment. Ifthere is a protest, it will be assigned to a 
Hearing Officer. After the conclusion of the protest process and hearing, the Hearing Officer closes 
the case by issuing a Notice of Action to affirm, revise, or withdraw the proposed assessment. The 
taxpayer then has 30 days to appeal to the State Board of Equalization. 
The audit of the Party has recently concluded. The Audit Division's detennination, that 
assessments be proposed, is to be issued soon. As such, the protest process has not yet begun. 
From time to time during the course of an audit, one or more members of the FTB's Audit Division 
may consult with a member of the FTB's Legal Division for technical guidance. However, the 
Audit Division maintains ultimate authority over whether an assessment will be issued. In this case, 
the Audit Division contacted an FTB staff attorney for such guidance. 
• On September 30, 2013, an email was sent to Mr. Joseph by a FTB staff attorney regarding 
the audit. The staff attorney's inquiry to Mr. Joseph was prompted by her supervisor who, 
according to the email, had previously mentioned the matter to Mr. Joseph. The staff 
attorney's supervisor directed the staff attorney to ask Mr. Joseph for his input. 
• In the email, the FTB staff attorney asked Mr. Joseph to give his opinion regarding the staff 
attorney's approach to analyzing the case. In that regard, she set forth the facts at issue and 
the approach she was considering using to analyze the matter. More precisely, the attorney 
asked Mr. Joseph "What is your reaction on my initial take on this one?" Mr. Joseph 
responded to the email by giving input on what issues the staff attorney should analyze in 
order to reach a conclusion. He also recommended that the staff attorney review various 
cases. 
• The email response is set forth in three paragraphs, concluding with Mr. Joseph stating "I 
think••••• , but please read the••••• case and even the ..... to get a sense as to how the law 
works on ...... in this context."2 You stated that Mr. Joseph did not give an opinion as to 
what the correct final detennination should be regarding the issues raised the email. 
The FTB's Audit Division continued the audit over the next three years, and a final 
detennination in the audit was not reached until 2016, well after Mr. Joseph left state service. 
V You noted that the FTB pro\'ided Mr. Joseph with a copy of the email regarding the audit on November l. 
2016. You also stated that the FTB staff deemed the email to be attorney work product and an attorney client privileged 
communication. As such., the actual language of the email itself is not included in this request, but rather described in 
general terms. 


ANALYSIS 
File No. A-16-244 
Page No. 3 
Public officials who leave state service are subject to two types of post-governmental 
employment provisions under the Act, the "one-year ban" and the "pennanent ban," colloquially 
known as the "revolving door'' prohibitions. Your question concerns the "pennanent ban" of 
Sections 87400 et seq. as they apply to Mr. Joseph's former position at the FTB. The "pennanent 
ban" prohibits a former state employee from ''switching sides" and participating, for compensation, 
in any specific proceeding involving the State of California if the proceeding is one in which the 
former state employee participated while employed by the state. (See Sections 87401-87402, 
Regulation 18741.1.) 
The pcnnanent ban applies to every "state administrative official," which is defined as 
"every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state administrative agency who as part of his 
or her official responsibilities engages in any judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding in other 
than a purely clerical, secretarial or ministerial capacity." (Section 87400(b).) The pennanent ban is 
a lifetime ban and applies to any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding in which a state 
administrative official participated while he or she served as a state administrative official. 
"Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding" means any proceeding, application, request for a 
ruling or other determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation, arrest 
or other particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any court or state administrative 
agency." (Section 87400(c).) 
You do not question whether Mr. Joseph is subject to the permanent ban or whether the 
audit is a quasi-judicial, or other proceeding subject to the ban. Rather you argue that Mr. Joseph 
never participated in the proceeding as contemplated by the Act. Section 87400(d) defines 
"participated" as "to have taken part personally and substantially through decision, approval, 
disapproval, formal written recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis, investigation 
or use of confidential information as an officer or employee, but excluding approval, disapproval or 
rendering of legal advisory opinions to departmental or agency staff which do not involve a specific 
party or parties." 
With respect to this definition, you contend that Mr. Joseph did not "participate" in the 
proceeding for the following reasons: 
( l) You note Mr. Joseph was not serving in the supervisory chain of either the Audit 
Division (which was actually conducting the proceeding in question) or the Legal Division of the 
FTB. Therefore, you state that the prior rulings of the FPPC regarding activities in the supervisory 
chain of a proceeding are not applicable in this matter. We do not disagree with this factual 
statement, and we have not applied the rules applicable to supervisors. 
(2) You also suggest that since Mr. Joseph was not a supervisor, it must be concluded that 
there was significant intervening substantive review of any legal advice that he provided. However, 
this is a mere supposition and an insufficient basis for immunizing advice. Considering that 
management referred the legal question to Mr. Joseph, it is equally possible that Mr. Joseph's legal 
advice guided the proceeding to its ultimate conclusion. Based on the one redacted email you 
provided, we are unable to make any determination on this factual question. 


File No. A-16-244 
Page No. 4 
(3) You stated that Mr. Joseph did not take part "substantially" in the proceeding. You stated 
that his input was slight given the many years the proceeding was ongoing, and he made no 
decision or approval regarding the proceeding. Further, his input did not reach any conclusions. 
Rather, he pointed another attorney to some cases that might bear on advice she would eventually 
give to the auditors in question. 
With respect to the application of the law, the statute is not solely concerned with making a 
decision in a proceeding. Rather, "participation" includes formal written recommendations and 
rendering advice on a substantial basis. Section 87400 does not define the term "substantial."3 
However, the email, even with information redacted, appears to be a legal opinion of substance. It 
provided legal advke with respect to the audit that the agency considers to be work product and 
protected as an attorney client privileged communication. 
Moreover, we also note that the statute makes specific reference to "legal opinions" in the 
context of"participation." Section 87400(d) excludes legal advisory opinions so long as the 
opinions do not involve a specific party or parties. It is a fundamental rule of statutory construction 
that where there arc stated exceptions to a general rule, it will be presumed that the Legislature 
intended no exceptions beyond that specified. (Stock/011 Theatres. /11c. 1·. Palermo ( 1956) 47 Cal.2d 
469, 476). It is clear that the questi.ons at issue involved a specific audit that concerned specific 
parties of whom Mr. Joseph was apparently aware. 
Further, the email and the FTB's treatment of the email also suggest that the tontent of the 
email included facts about the audit known to Mr. Joseph that would not have been known by the 
public. Using confidential facts now for the benefit of a private employer would appear to be the 
exact "switching sides" that the statute seeks to prevent. 
We note that the detennination of whether a fonner state employee participated in a 
proceeding is necessarily a factual one, and that you have provided limited facts. The Act does 
provide a process for the factual determination of whether a former employee should be excluded 
from a proceeding pursuant to Sections 87400 et seq. Section 87404 provides: 
"Upon the petition of any interested person or party, the court or the 
presiding or other officer, including but not limited to a hearing officer serving 
pursuant to Section 11512 of the Government Code. in any judicial. quasi-judicial 
or other proceeding, including but not limited to any proceeding pursuant to 
Chapter 5 (commencing with Section 11500) of Part I of Division 3 of Title 2 of 
the Government Code may, after notice and an opportunity for a hearing, exclude 
any person found to be in violation of this article from further participation, or 
from assisting or counseling any other participant, in the proceeding then pending 
before such court or presiding or other officer." 
Section 87404 does not direct or authorize the Commission to participate in a detennination under 
this Section. 
J Stacks Law Dictionary defines the term to mean significant or large and ha,ing substance. Oxford Dictionary 
defines "substantially" as: "to a great or significant extent." 


File No. A-16-244 
Page No. 5 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
JWW:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
eneral Counsel, 
ision

QUESTION: ls Mr. Joseph, a former employee of the Franchise Tax Board ("FTB") and currently a 
Principal at Ernst & Young, LLP (hereafter "E&Y"), subject to the permanent ban provisions of 
Sections 87401-87402 in relation to a specific audit that now is subject to protest?

prohibit Mr. Joseph from participating in the audit proceeding. However, Section 87404 does 
provide for a process to allow an agency and former employee to determine factually if a former 
employee should be excluded from a proceeding pursuant to Section 87400 et seq. Section 87404 
does not direct or authorize the Commission to participate in the determination.
